---
title: "Curve Fitting"
author: "Katrin Kulberg, Maximilian Brunner, Salomé Moret, Louis del Perugia"
date: "03.04.2026"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)

# libraries
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(MASS)
library(coda)
```

## Data Preprocessing

```{r}
# loading data
load("data/speaker1_tongue_shapes.RData")
load("data/speaker2_tongue_shapes.RData")

# transforming data into a data frame shape
# first speaker
df1 <- data.frame(
  x = as.vector(speaker1[, 1, ]), # Store the 1st column as a vector for the x
  y = as.vector(speaker1[, 2, ]), # Store the 2nd column as a vector for the y
  t = rep(1:dim(speaker1)[1], times = dim(speaker1)[3]), # Indice of the point along the curve (65 per shape)
  shape = rep(1:dim(speaker1)[3], each = dim(speaker1)[1]), # Indice of the shape
  speaker = 1
)

# second speaker
df2 <- data.frame(
  x = as.vector(speaker2[, 1, ]), # Store the 1st column as a vector for the x
  y = as.vector(speaker2[, 2, ]), # Store the 2nd column as a vector for the y
  t = rep(1:dim(speaker2)[1], times = dim(speaker2)[3]), # Indice of the point along the curve (65 per shape)
  shape = rep(1:dim(speaker2)[3], each = dim(speaker2)[1]), # Indice of the shape for each point
  speaker = 2
)

# reordering the indices t for speaker 2
df2$t <- 45 - df2$t + 1 # The points are in the "wrong" order, last point is the first one

# Given that the 5th shape was already in the right order, reordering that shape again
# Additionally removing an outlier from shape 5
df2 <- df2 %>%
  mutate(t = ifelse(shape == 5, 45 - t + 1, t)) %>%
  filter(!(shape == 5 & t == 45))


# Adding standardised index t to both data frames
# (multiplying by 1/(n+1))
df1$t_standardised <- df1$t / 66
df2$t_standardised <- df2$t / 46
```

## Descriptive Overview

```{r}
# FULL CURVES

# curves of speaker 1
ggplot(df1, aes(x = x, y = y)) + #
  geom_point(size = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~shape, ncol = 5) +
  coord_fixed() +
  theme_bw()

# curves of speaker 2
ggplot(df2, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(size = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~shape, ncol = 5) +
  coord_fixed() +
  theme_bw()

# MARGINAL CURVES

# speaker 1, x curve
ggplot(df1, aes(x = t, y = x)) +
  geom_point(size = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~shape, ncol = 5) +
  coord_fixed() +
  theme_bw()

# speaker 1, y curve
ggplot(df1, aes(x = t, y = y)) +
  geom_point(size = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~shape, ncol = 5) +
  coord_fixed() +
  theme_bw()

# speaker 2, x curve
ggplot(df2, aes(x = t, y = x)) +
  geom_point(size = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~shape, ncol = 5) +
  coord_fixed() +
  theme_bw()

# speaker 2, y curve
ggplot(df2, aes(x = t, y = y)) +
  geom_point(size = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~shape, ncol = 5) +
  coord_fixed() +
  theme_bw()
```

# Function Definitions

```{r}
# Function that, for given x and y, calculates coefficients for the polynomial basis for a given degree
coefs <- function(x, y, degree) {
  # design matrix
  X_poly <- sapply(0:degree, function(i) x^i)
  # finding coefficients
  model <- lm(y ~ X_poly - 1)
  beta <- coef(model)

  return(beta)
}

# function that computes coefficients for the fully observed curves
# data frame, variable and degree can be specified
compute_betas <- function(df, variable, degree, shapes = 3:10) {
  betas_list <- lapply(shapes, function(i) {
    # selecting response
    y <- df[df$shape == i, variable]

    # based on standardised index t
    coefs(df[df$shape == i, "t_standardised"], y, degree)
  })

  do.call(rbind, betas_list)
}

# function that selects the best fitting curves based on observed data
# number of curves can be specified
curve_selection <- function(X_observed, y_observed, number = 6) {
  results <- matrix(NA, nrow = ncol(X_observed), ncol = 2)

  for (i in 1:ncol(X_observed)) {
    # similarity is based on squared difference
    similarity <- sum((X_observed[, i] - y_observed)^2)
    results[i, 1] <- i
    results[i, 2] <- similarity
  }

  # sorting by similarity in an ascending order
  results <- results[order(results[, 2]), ]

  # returning indices
  return(results[1:number, 1])
}

# function for Gibbs sampling in case of modelling x and y curves separately
Gibbs_1d <- function(x, y, coefs, observed, simulations) {
  # for testing purposes
  # x <- subset(df1, shape == 1)$t_standardised
  # coefs <- coefs_X
  # observed <- 21:65
  # y <- subset(df1, shape == 1)$x
  # simulations <- 5000

  # building design matrix
  X_poly <- sapply(0:(ncol(coefs) - 1), function(i) x^i)
  X_full <- t((coefs) %*% t(X_poly))

  # choosing the observed parts
  y_observed <- y[observed]
  X_observed <- X_full[observed, ]

  # curve selection
  selected <- curve_selection(X_observed, y_observed, ncol(coefs))
  X_observed <- X_observed[, selected]
  X_full <- X_full[, selected]


  # initialising parameters for priors
  p <- ncol(X_observed)
  beta0 <- rep(0.5, p)
  Sigma0_inv <- 1 / 1 * diag(1, p)
  n <- length(y_observed)

  # choose
  nu0 <- 2
  s20 <- 5

  beta_samples <- matrix(NA, nrow = simulations, ncol = p)
  sigma2_samples <- numeric(simulations)

  # setting initial values
  beta_samples[1, ] <- rep(1 / p, p)
  sigma2_samples[1] <- var(y_observed)
  n <- length(y_observed)

  for (iter in 2:simulations) {
    # update beta
    # finding covariance matrix
    V_beta <- solve(t(X_observed) %*% X_observed / sigma2_samples[iter - 1] + Sigma0_inv)
    # find mean vectors
    m_beta <- V_beta %*% (t(X_observed) %*% y_observed / sigma2_samples[iter - 1] + Sigma0_inv %*% beta0)

    # sampling
    # beta_samples[iter, ] <- m_beta + t(chol(V_beta)) %*% rnorm(p)
    beta_samples[iter, ] <- mvrnorm(1, mu = m_beta, Sigma = V_beta)

    # updating sigma^2

    # calculating SSR
    SSR <- sum((y_observed - X_observed %*% beta_samples[iter, ])^2)

    # parameters for inverse gamma
    shape_sig <- (nu0 + n) / 2
    rate_sig <- (nu0 * s20 + SSR) / 2

    # sampling
    sigma2_samples[iter] <- 1 / rgamma(1, shape = shape_sig, rate = rate_sig)
  }

  # predictions for the full curve
  predictions <- X_full %*% t(beta_samples)

  # returning predictions, betas and selected curves
  return(list(
    predictions = predictions,
    beta_samples = beta_samples,
    selected = selected
  ))
}

# function for Gibbs sampling in case of modelling x and y curves together
Gibbs_2d <- function(points, x, y, coefs_X, coefs_Y, observed, simulations) {
  # for testing purposes

  # points <- subset(df1, shape == 1)$t_standardised
  # points <- 1:65
  # coefs_X <- coefs_X_speaker1
  # coefs_Y <- coefs_Y_speaker1
  # observed <- c(1:19, 41:65)
  # observed <- 21:65
  # x <- subset(df1, shape == 1)$x
  # y <- subset(df1, shape == 1)$y
  # simulations <- 5000

  # building design matrix for x and y
  X_poly_x <- sapply(0:(ncol(coefs_X) - 1), function(i) points^i)
  X_poly_y <- sapply(0:(ncol(coefs_Y) - 1), function(i) points^i)
  X_full_x <- t((coefs_X) %*% t(X_poly_x))
  X_full_y <- t((coefs_Y) %*% t(X_poly_y))

  # combining x and y
  X_full <- rbind(X_full_x, X_full_y)

  # choosing the observed parts for both
  X_observed_x <- X_full_x[observed, ]
  X_observed_y <- X_full_y[observed, ]
  y_observed_x <- x[observed]
  y_observed_y <- y[observed]

  # combining the observed parts
  y_observed <- c(y_observed_x, y_observed_y)
  X_observed <- rbind(X_observed_x, X_observed_y)

  # curve selection (11 curves)
  selected <- curve_selection(X_observed, y_observed, (ncol(coefs_X) + ncol(coefs_Y)))
  X_observed <- X_observed[, selected]
  X_full <- X_full[, selected]


  # initialising parameters for priors
  p <- ncol(X_observed)
  beta0 <- rep(0.5, p)
  Sigma0_inv <- 1 / 1 * diag(1, p)

  # choose
  nu0 <- 2 * 2
  s20 <- 5 * 2.5
  n <- length(y_observed)

  beta_samples <- matrix(NA, nrow = simulations, ncol = p)
  sigma2_samples <- numeric(simulations)

  # initial values
  beta_samples[1, ] <- rep(1 / p, p)
  sigma2_samples[1] <- var(y_observed)

  for (iter in 2:simulations) {
    # updating beta
    # finding covariance matrix
    V_beta <- solve((t(X_observed) %*% X_observed) / sigma2_samples[iter - 1] + Sigma0_inv)

    # finding mean vectors
    m_beta <- V_beta %*% ((t(X_observed) %*% y_observed) / sigma2_samples[iter - 1] + Sigma0_inv %*% beta0)

    # sampling beta
    # beta_samples[iter, ] <- m_beta + t(chol(V_beta)) %*% rnorm(p)
    beta_samples[iter, ] <- mvrnorm(1, mu = m_beta, Sigma = V_beta)

    # updating sigma^2

    # SSR
    SSR <- sum((y_observed - X_observed %*% beta_samples[iter, ])^2)

    # parameters for inverse gamma
    shape_sig <- (nu0 + n) / 2
    rate_sig <- (nu0 * s20 + SSR) / 2

    # sampling
    sigma2_samples[iter] <- 1 / rgamma(1, shape = shape_sig, rate = rate_sig)
  }

  # predictions for the full curve
  predictions <- X_full %*% t(beta_samples)

  # returning predictions, betas and selected curves
  return(list(
    predictions = predictions,
    beta_samples = beta_samples,
    selected = selected
  ))
}

# function that plots the curve with credible interval in 1D
summarise_results_1d <- function(predictions, points, true_data) {
  # summarising as a data frame
  df <- data.frame(
    best_estimate = apply(predictions, 1, quantile, probs = 0.5),
    lower_95 = apply(predictions, 1, quantile, probs = 0.025),
    upper_95 = apply(predictions, 1, quantile, probs = 0.975)
  )

  # plotting
  p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = points)) +
    geom_line(aes(y = best_estimate), color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
    geom_line(aes(y = lower_95), color = "black", linetype = "dashed") +
    geom_line(aes(y = upper_95), color = "black", linetype = "dashed") +
    geom_point(aes(y = true_data), color = "red") +
    labs(x = "t", y = "x") +
    theme_bw()

  return(p)
}

# function that plots the curve with credible interval in 2D
summarise_results_2d <- function(predictions_x, predictions_y, points, true_data_x, true_data_y) {
  # testing
  # predictions_x <- predictions_x_speaker1_curve1
  # predictions_y <- predictions_y_speaker1_curve1

  # points <- 1:65
  # true_data_x <- subset(df1, df1$shape == 2)$x
  # true_data_y <- subset(df1, df1$shape == 2)$y

  # summarising as a data frame
  df_xy <- data.frame(
    best_estimate_x = apply(predictions_x, 1, quantile, probs = 0.5),
    lower_95_x = apply(predictions_x, 1, quantile, probs = 0.025),
    upper_95_x = apply(predictions_x, 1, quantile, probs = 0.975),
    best_estimate_y = apply(predictions_y, 1, quantile, probs = 0.5),
    lower_95_y = apply(predictions_y, 1, quantile, probs = 0.025),
    upper_95_y = apply(predictions_y, 1, quantile, probs = 0.975)
  )


  # plotting
  # (IN PROGRESS but the current version works as a quick sanity check)
  p <- ggplot() +
    geom_rect(
      data = df_xy,
      aes(
        xmin = lower_95_x,
        xmax = upper_95_x,
        ymin = lower_95_y,
        ymax = upper_95_y
      ),
      fill = "grey70", alpha = 0.3
    ) +
    # these rectangles do not model correctly the joint credible interval technically in case of 2d
    # since rectangles essentially correspond to independent x and y (so 1st case is ok)
    # best estimate
    geom_path(
      data = df_xy,
      aes(x = best_estimate_x, y = best_estimate_y),
      color = "black", linewidth = 1.2
    ) +
    geom_point(aes(x = true_data_x, y = true_data_y), color = "red") +
    theme_bw() +
    labs(x = "x", y = "y")

  return(p)
}
```


# Conjugate model (example)

```{r}
# without sampling sigma^2
# could be done for both 1D and 2D.
# as an example, here is for 1D, speaker 1, x

# setting degrees
degree_x <- 5
degree_y <- 4

coefs_X_speaker1 <- compute_betas(df1, "x", degree_x)
coefs_Y_speaker1 <- compute_betas(df1, "y", degree_y)

coefs_X_speaker2 <- compute_betas(df2, "x", degree_x)
coefs_Y_speaker2 <- compute_betas(df2, "y", degree_y)

######################################################

conjugate_model_1d <- function(x, y, coefs, observed, samples) {
  X_poly <- sapply(0:(ncol(coefs) - 1), function(i) x^i)
  X_full <- t((coefs) %*% t(X_poly))

  y_observed <- y[observed]
  X_observed <- X_full[observed, ]

  # curve selection
  selected <- curve_selection(X_observed, y_observed, ncol(coefs))
  X_observed <- X_observed[, selected]
  X_full <- X_full[, selected]

  # initialising parameters for priors
  p <- ncol(X_observed)
  # priors
  beta0 <- rep(0.5, p)
  Sigma0_inv <- 1 / 0.5 * diag(1, p)
  sigma2 <- 2 # FIXED! really depends on this; this can be investigated further which choice makes the most sense
  n <- length(y_observed)

  # calculating covariance and mean
  V_beta <- solve(t(X_observed) %*% X_observed / sigma2 + Sigma0_inv)
  m_beta <- V_beta %*% (t(X_observed) %*% y_observed / sigma2 + Sigma0_inv %*% beta0)

  # sampling
  beta_samples <- mvrnorm(samples, mu = m_beta, Sigma = V_beta)

  predictions <- X_full %*% t(beta_samples)

  return(predictions)
}


curve_1x_speaker1 <- conjugate_model_1d(subset(df1, shape == 1)$t_standardised, subset(df1, shape == 1)$x, coefs_X_speaker1, 21:65, 5000)
summarise_results_1d(curve_1x_speaker1, 1:65, subset(df1, shape == 1)$x)
```


# 1D approaches

```{r}
# modelling x and y curves separately

# setting degrees
degree_x <- 5
degree_y <- 4

coefs_X_speaker1 <- compute_betas(df1, "x", degree_x)
coefs_Y_speaker1 <- compute_betas(df1, "y", degree_y)

coefs_X_speaker2 <- compute_betas(df2, "x", degree_x)
coefs_Y_speaker2 <- compute_betas(df2, "y", degree_y)

#####################################################

# Gibbs sampling procedures
# SPEAKER 1
# observed portion for curve 1: 21:65
# observed portion for curve 2: c(1:19, 41:65)
speaker_1_shape_1_x <- Gibbs_1d(subset(df1, shape == 1)$t_standardised, subset(df1, shape == 1)$x, coefs_X_speaker1, 21:65, 5000)
summarise_results_1d(speaker_1_shape_1_x$predictions, 1:65, subset(df1, shape == 1)$x)

speaker_1_shape_1_y <- Gibbs_1d(subset(df1, shape == 1)$t_standardised, subset(df1, shape == 1)$y, coefs_Y_speaker1, 21:65, 5000)
summarise_results_1d(speaker_1_shape_1_y$predictions, 1:65, subset(df1, shape == 1)$y)

# 2d visualisation
summarise_results_2d(speaker_1_shape_1_x$predictions, speaker_1_shape_1_y$predictions, 1:65, subset(df1, shape == 1)$x, subset(df1, shape == 1)$y)

speaker_1_shape_2_x <- Gibbs_1d(subset(df1, shape == 2)$t_standardised, subset(df1, shape == 2)$x, coefs_X_speaker1, c(1:19, 41:65), 5000)
summarise_results_1d(speaker_1_shape_2_x$predictions, 1:65, subset(df1, shape == 2)$x)


speaker_1_shape_2_y <- Gibbs_1d(subset(df1, shape == 2)$t_standardised, subset(df1, shape == 2)$y, coefs_Y_speaker1, c(1:19, 41:65), 5000)
summarise_results_1d(speaker_1_shape_2_y$predictions, 1:65, subset(df1, shape == 2)$y)


# SPEAKER 2
# observed portion for curve 1: 21:45
# observed portion for curve 2: c(1:19, 36:45)

speaker_2_shape_1_x <- Gibbs_1d(subset(df2, shape == 1)$t_standardised, subset(df2, shape == 1)$x, coefs_X_speaker2, 21:45, 5000)
summarise_results_1d(speaker_2_shape_1_x$predictions, rev(1:45), subset(df2, shape == 1)$x)


speaker_2_shape_1_y <- Gibbs_1d(subset(df2, shape == 1)$t_standardised, subset(df2, shape == 1)$y, coefs_Y_speaker2, 21:45, 5000)
summarise_results_1d(speaker_2_shape_1_y$predictions, rev(1:45), subset(df2, shape == 1)$y)

summarise_results_2d(speaker_2_shape_1_x$predictions, speaker_2_shape_1_y$predictions, rev(1:65), subset(df2, shape == 1)$x, subset(df2, shape == 1)$y)


speaker_2_shape_2_x <- Gibbs_1d(subset(df2, shape == 2)$t_standardised, subset(df2, shape == 2)$x, coefs_X_speaker2, c(1:19, 36:45), 5000)
summarise_results_1d(speaker_2_shape_2_x$predictions, rev(1:45), subset(df2, shape == 2)$x)


speaker_2_shape_2_y <- Gibbs_1d(subset(df2, shape == 2)$t_standardised, subset(df2, shape == 2)$y, coefs_Y_speaker2, c(1:19, 36:45), 5000)
summarise_results_1d(speaker_2_shape_2_y$predictions, rev(1:45), subset(df2, shape == 2)$y)
```


# 2D approaches

```{r}
# modelling x and y curves together
# note that here we allow to select from both speaker 1 and speaker 2
# that is because we need 11 curves (6+5 parameters in total)

# setting degrees
degree_x <- 5
degree_y <- 4

coefs_X_speaker1 <- compute_betas(df1, "x", degree_x)
coefs_Y_speaker1 <- compute_betas(df1, "y", degree_y)

coefs_X_speaker2 <- compute_betas(df2, "x", degree_x)
coefs_Y_speaker2 <- compute_betas(df2, "y", degree_y)


# combining information from speaker 1 and 2
coefs_X <- rbind(coefs_X_speaker1, coefs_X_speaker2)
coefs_Y <- rbind(coefs_Y_speaker1, coefs_Y_speaker2)

#######################################################
# Gibbs sampling procedures
# SPEAKER 1
# observed portion for curve 1: 21:65
# observed portion for curve 2: c(1:19, 41:65)

speaker1_curve1 <- Gibbs_2d(subset(df1, shape == 1)$t_standardised, subset(df1, shape == 1)$x, subset(df1, shape == 1)$y, coefs_X, coefs_Y, 21:65, simulations)

summarise_results_1d(speaker1_curve1$predictions[1:65, ], 1:65, subset(df1, shape == 1)$x)

summarise_results_2d(speaker1_curve1$predictions[1:65, ], speaker1_curve1$predictions[66:130, ], 1:65, subset(df1, shape == 1)$x, subset(df1, shape == 1)$y)

speaker1_curve2 <- Gibbs_2d(subset(df1, shape == 2)$t_standardised, subset(df1, shape == 2)$x, subset(df1, shape == 2)$y, coefs_X, coefs_Y, c(1:19, 41:65), simulations)

summarise_results_1d(speaker1_curve2$predictions[1:65, ], 1:65, subset(df1, shape == 2)$x)

summarise_results_2d(speaker1_curve2$predictions[1:65, ], speaker1_curve2$predictions[66:130, ], 1:65, subset(df1, shape == 2)$x, subset(df1, shape == 2)$y)

# SPEAKER 2
# observed portion for curve 1: 21:45
# observed portion for curve 2: c(1:19, 36:45)

speaker2_curve1 <- Gibbs_2d(subset(df2, shape == 1)$t_standardised, subset(df2, shape == 1)$x, subset(df2, shape == 1)$y, coefs_X, coefs_Y, 21:45, simulations)

summarise_results_1d(speaker2_curve1$predictions[1:45, ], rev(1:45), subset(df2, shape == 1)$x)

summarise_results_2d(speaker2_curve1$predictions[1:45, ], speaker2_curve1$predictions[46:90, ], 1:45, subset(df2, shape == 1)$x, subset(df2, shape == 1)$y)


speaker2_curve2 <- Gibbs_2d(subset(df2, shape == 2)$t_standardised, subset(df2, shape == 2)$x, subset(df2, shape == 2)$y, coefs_X, coefs_Y, c(1:19, 36:45), simulations)

summarise_results_2d(speaker2_curve2$predictions[1:45, ], speaker2_curve2$predictions[46:90, ], 1:45, subset(df2, shape == 2)$x, subset(df2, shape == 2)$y)
```

# Diagnostics MCMC
```{r}
# Let us define our diagnostics MCMC function
mcmc_diag <- function(Gibbs_Output, burnin){
  # First, extract the samples matrice
  beta_samples <- as.matrix(Gibbs_Output$beta_samples)
  
  # Store the dimensions, S is the number of iterations, P the number of parameters
  S <- nrow(beta_samples)
  p <- ncol(beta_samples)
  
  # Check that burn-in is smaller than the number of iterations
  if (burnin >= S){
    stop("The burnin (", burnin, "is bigger than the total number of samples", S)
  }
  
  # Now, apply the burn-in
  beta_sampclean <- beta_samples[(burnin+1):S, ,drop=FALSE]
  
  # TRACEPLOT:
  # Let's do a Traceplot for each beta to check the MCMC
  param_names <- paste0("Beta_", 1:p) # Will be used for the name of traceplot
  # Just to be readable, we put 3 graphs per row.
  n_rowgraph <- ceiling(p/3)
  n_colgraph <- 3 
  par(mfrow = c(n_rowgraph, n_colgraph))
  
  for (j in 1:p){ # for each parameter
    plot(1:S, beta_samples[,j], type="l", main = paste("Traceplot of", param_names[j]), xlab = "iterations", ylab = "value of beta", col = "blue1")
    abline(v = burnin, col = "red2", lty = 2) # Add a vertical line at the burn-in
  }

  
  # AUTOCORRELATION FUNCTION:
  # Now, let's plot the autocorrelation functions
  for (j in 1:p){
    acf(beta_sampclean[,j], main = paste("ACF of", param_names[j]), lag.max = 50, cex.main = 0.9)
  }
  
  # EFFECTIVE SAMPLE SIZE
  # We use the coda package, but first we have to convert beta_sampclean into an mcmc object
  beta_mcmc <- as.mcmc(beta_sampclean)
  colnames(beta_mcmc) <- param_names
  
  # Now, we can compute the effective sample size for each parameter
  ess <- effectiveSize(beta_mcmc)
  print(paste("Minimum ESS", min(ess)))
  print(paste("Mean ESS", mean(ess)))
  print(round(ess, 1))
  
  # Return invisibly a list of ess and corresponding beat_sampclean
  invisible(list(ess = ess, beta_sampclean = beta_sampclean))
}
```

# Cell to see the output !
```{r}
speaker_1_shape_1_x <- Gibbs_1d(
  subset(df1, shape == 1)$t_standardised,
  subset(df1, shape == 1)$x,
  coefs_X_speaker1, 21:65, 5000
)

res <- mcmc_diag(speaker_1_shape_1_x, 500)
res$ess
```
